steps.title: Identify the main verb and its required subject
steps.content: The word bestows is clearly the main verb, appearing with objects and prepositional phrases at the sentence's end. The subject must begin with “Helical” and come before “bestows.” Between the potential subject and verb stands the phrase “widely understood to confer stability and efficiency in the locomotion of a variety of microscopic organisms—including bacteria, eukaryotic algae, and ciliates,” which is a participial phrase (a modifier using the past participle “understood”)—not a second main verb.

steps.title: Apply the punctuation rule for intervening modifiers
steps.content: When a participial phrase interrupts between a subject and its main verb, it must be set off by punctuation. Choice D, “swimming,” creates the subject “Helical swimming” followed by a comma that opens the participial phrase “widely understood to confer...ciliates,” which closes with the em dash before the main verb “bestows,” establishing the proper [Subject], [participial phrase—], [main verb] structure. Choices A, B, and C each insert a verb (“is,” “has been”) into the modifier, creating two independent clauses that cannot be properly joined. That matches choice D — D is correct.
